content:
9 min read
Just now

--

I wrapped every function in useCallback for 3 months. My code was slower, harder to read, and my senior dev sent me a PR review that just said: “Why?”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Hook: My useCallback Addiction
Let me tell you about the most embarrassing code review of my 6-month career.
I had just learned about useMemo and useCallback. I was pumped. I finally understood what "memoization" meant, and I was going to use it everywhere. Every variable? useMemo. Every function? useCallback. Every component? React.memo.
I submitted a PR for a simple user list component. It had 47 lines of logic. It had 6 useMemo calls and 4 useCallback calls. It was a masterpiece of optimization.
My senior dev’s review comment:
“Why did you wrap const name = 'John' in useMemo?"
I had literally memoized a string literal.
That was the day I learned that premature optimization isn’t just wasteful — it actively makes your code worse. And that most React developers (especially juniors like me) are using useMemo and useCallback completely wrong.
Let’s fix that.
Part 1: What Is a Re-Render, Really?
